perturbation.original_text: EFCA agrees to keep the existence and nature of this Agreement confidential and not to use the same or the name of the disclosing party (or of any company in the Group of Companies of which the disclosing party forms part) in any public ity, adv ertisement or other disclosure with regard to this Agreement without the pr ior written consent of the disclos ing party.
perturbation.changed_text: EFCA agrees to keep the existence and nature of this Agreement confidential and not to use the same or the name of the disclosing party (or of any company in the Group of Companies of which the disclosing party forms part) in any publicity, advertisement, or other disclosure with regard to this Agreement, and such consent by the disclosing party is not required.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for prior written consent to use the disclosing party's name in publicity materials could violate laws protecting trademarks and business reputation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 120 of the Trade Marks Act 1995 prohibits the use of a registered trade mark by unauthorized parties, and this modification allows EFCA to use the disclosing party's name without consent, which could infringe on their trademark rights.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for prior written consent, potentially allowing EFCA to use the disclosing party's name in ways that could infringe on trademark rights. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight this potential conflict with trademark law, even though Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly address trademark law but provides context that laws exist, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 provides no information.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice to be given under this Agr eement shall be in writing and sen t by facsimile transmission or s ent by re corded delivery post to the receiving party at its business address as last not ified in writing to the other party and shall be deemed to have been given on the date of the facsimile transmission or two days following that on w hich the notice was posted.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice to be given under this Agreement shall be in writing and sent exclusively by carrier pigeon to the receiving party at its business address as last notified in writing to the other party and shall be deemed to have been given on the third Tuesday of the following month.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting notice to 'carrier pigeon' and delaying it until the 'third Tuesday of the following month' is unreasonable and likely unenforceable under general contract law principles requiring reasonable notice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Requirement for Reasonable Notice
perturbation.law_citation: Implied duty of good faith and fair dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The implied duty of good faith and fair dealing requires parties to a contract to act honestly and reasonably. Requiring notice by carrier pigeon is not a reasonable form of communication, especially in modern business practices, and the delay until the 'third Tuesday of the following month' is also an unreasonable timeframe.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an unreasonable method of communication (carrier pigeon) and an unreasonably delayed timeframe (third Tuesday of the following month) for providing notice, which contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "good faith" as encompassing "honest dealing" and "observance of fair dealing standards." The modified text's notice provisions are unlikely to be considered honest or fair in a modern business context. Therefore, the modification creates a legal contradiction by undermining the obligation to act in good faith.

perturbation.original_text: This Agree ment constitutes the entire Agreement and under standing between the parties in respect of C onfidentia l Information and supersedes all previous agreement s, understandings and undertakings in suc h respect. This Agreement cannot be c hanged except by wr itten agreement between t he parties. The inter pretation, construction and effect of this Agreement shall be enforced in accor danc e with the laws of the State of Victoria.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire Agreement and understanding between the parties in respect of Confidential Information and supersedes all previous agreements, understandings, and undertakings in such respect. This Agreement can be changed orally by either party. The interpretation, construction, and effect of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of North Korea.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing oral modifications contradicts standard 'no oral modification' clauses, and specifying North Korean law is likely unenforceable and against public policy given the agreement is between Australian entities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Frauds (regarding enforceability of oral modifications) and Public Policy
perturbation.law_citation: General Contract Law - Statute of Frauds
perturbation.law_explanation: While Australia doesn't have a single Statute of Frauds law like in the US, the principle that certain contracts (especially those lasting over a year) must be in writing is a common law principle to prevent fraudulent claims. Furthermore, choosing North Korean law could violate public policy because it could be seen as against fundamental principles of fairness and justice as applied in Australia.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: statute of frauds | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you statute of frauds Statute of frauds is a statute requiring certain contracts to be in writing and signed by the parties bound by the contract. The purpose is to prevent fraud and other injury. The most common types of contracts to which the statute applies are contracts that involve the sale or transfer of land, (see: real estate transactions ), and contracts that cannot be completed within one year. See also: Y amaha Motor Corp., U.S.A. v. Calhoun , 516 U.S. 199 (1996) Contracts for the sale of goods worth $500 or more. UCC § 2-201 [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities finance financial services housing PROPERTY landlord & tenant property & real estate law business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions property law statutes Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces two significant contradictions. First, it allows for oral modifications of the agreement, directly contradicting the standard 'no oral modification' clauses common in contract law, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. This is further supported by the Law Explanation, which notes that certain contracts must be in writing to prevent fraudulent claims. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that the statute of frauds requires certain contracts to be in writing to prevent fraud, and while this snippet references US law, the underlying principle applies to Australian contract law as well. Second, the changed text specifies that the agreement shall be governed by the laws of North Korea. The Perturbation and Law Explanations both note that this choice of law is likely unenforceable and against public policy due to its conflict with fundamental principles of fairness and justice as applied in Australia. This undermines the enforceability and legal standing of the entire contract.

perturbation.original_text: This agree ment will b e effective as of the date below, and contin ue for five (5) years.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement will be effective as of the date below, and continue in perpetuity.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the agreement to last 'in perpetuity' could be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable, particularly if it restricts trade or limits a party's ability to engage in business activities indefinitely.
perturbation.law_explanation: Australian law, like many common law jurisdictions, disfavors agreements that unreasonably restrain trade. A confidentiality agreement lasting 'in perpetuity' could be challenged if it prevents a party from using general knowledge or skills acquired during the agreement, thereby hindering their ability to compete in the future.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a duration of five years, but the changed contract states the agreement continues 'in perpetuity'. The Law Explanation states that Australian law, like many common law jurisdictions, disfavors agreements that unreasonably restrain trade. Because 'in perpetuity' could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, this creates a legal contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a collection of links to legal articles, but does not include the actual legal text. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing. Therefore, the Law Explanation must be relied upon.
